coredata.dc:title: One-pot solid phase pyrolysis synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots and the interaction with human serum albumin
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xingjia
Xu, Liping
Zhang, Lizhi
Wang, Hongyan
Wang, Xiangming
Liu, Xuehui
Yao, Jie
Hao, Aijun
coredata.dc:description: One kind of fluorescent nitrogen–doped carbon dots (denoted as NCDs) was successfully fabricated by a simple and facile one–pot solid phase pyrolysis process using citric acid and glycine as raw materials. The obtained NCDs under the optimal synthetic conditions have a fluorescence quantum yield up to 35.6% and show a spherical shape with an average diameter of about 3.0nm. Moreover, NCDs can emit strong blue fluorescence and the surfaces when excited at 345nm and their surfaces were decorated with lots of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups. In order to further elucidate the effect of NCDs to protein, steady state/time-resolved fluorescence, UV–vis absorption, circular dichroism (CD), and Raman spectroscopic techniques as well as cyclic voltammetry (CV) were employed to explore the interaction of the as-synthesized NCDs with human serum albumin (HSA) in the simulated physiological environment. The experimental data reveal that NCDs could quench the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA via the formation of a ground-state complex with the association constants of the order of 104 L/mol. The values of thermodynamic parameters obtained at three different temperatures suggest that the binding reaction of NCDs with HSA mainly driven by hydrophobic forces and hydrogen bonds was spontaneous. Furthermore, the displacement experiments confirm that the binding of NCDs primarily took place in site I of HSA. The average distance between NCDs and tryptophan (Trp) residue of HSA was estimated to be 2.3nm according to the theory of Förster non-radiation energy transfer. Finally, the analysis of synchronous fluorescence, three-dimensional fluorescence (3D), Raman, and CD spectra manifests that HSA underwent some conformational changes by unfolding the polypeptides of protein and increasing the polarity of the microenvironment surrounding both Trp and tyrosine (Tyr) residues in the presence of NCDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nitrogen–doped carbon dots
Human serum albumin
Spectroscopic methods
Interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectral and electrochemical measurements for the interaction between NCDs and HSA
Morphology and structure of the prepared NCDs
Analysis of fluorescence quenching mechanism of HSA by NCDs
Binding constants and number of binding sites for the binding of NCDs to HSA
Localization of NCDs binding site on HSA
The polarity of the region around Trp and Tyr in HSA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Innovation Team Projects
Shenyang Science and Technology Plan Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support of Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Innovation Team Projects (Grant no. LT2012001 ), Shenyang Science and Technology Plan Project (Grant no. F13289100 ) and China Association for Medical Education ( 2016SKT-M019 ). The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (left) and the corresponding size distribution histogram (right) of the as-synthesized NCDs.
XRD pattern of NCDs.
FTIR spectrum of NCDs.
XPS spectrum of NCDs (a), C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) spectra of NCDs.
UV–vis absorption, emission and excitation spectra of NCDs aqueous solution.
PL spectra of NCDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths (from 290 to 390nm with a 10nm increment).

Fluorescence quenching spectra of HSA after adding different concentrations of NCDs (T = 310K, pH = 7.40 and λex = 295nm). [HSA] = 2.41×10−6 mol/L; [NCDs] (from a to g) = 0, 3.04, 6.08, 9.12, 12.16, 15.20 and 18.24×10−6 mol/L, respectively.
Stern–Volmer plots of HSA–NCDs system at three different temperatures. [HSA] was fixed at 2.41×10−6 mol/L.
Stern–Volmer plots of the HSA–NCDs system at two different concentrations of HSA at 298K. [HSA] = 3.6 and 4.8×10−6 mol/L, respectively.

Absorption spectra of NCDs, HSA (a), and the difference absorption spectra between HSA + NCDs and NCDs (from b to g). [HSA] = 2.0×10−6 mol/L; [NCDs] (from b to g) = 3.0, 6.1, 9.1, 12.2, 15.2 and 18.2×10−6 mol/L, respectively.

Time–resolved fluorescence decays of HSA after adding different amounts of NCDs. (a): [HSA] = 2.41×10−6 mol/L; (b) and (c): [HSA] = 2.41×10−6 mol/L and [CDs] (from b to c) = 2.41×10−6 and 4.82×10−6 mol/L, respectively.
CV curves of bare GCE and NCDs/GCE in the presence of different concentrations of HSA. [HSA] (from a to e) = 0, 0.12, 0.24, 0.36 and 0.48×10−6 mol/L, respectively.
Plots of lg(F0-F)/F versus lg[NCDs] for the HSA–NCDs system at three different temperatures.
Van’t Hoff plot of the HSA–NCDs system.
Spectral overlap of absorption spectrum of NCDs (b) with fluorescence emission spectrum of HSA (a). [HSA] = [NCDs] = 2.41×10−6 mol/L.
Effects of site maker probes on the fluorescence of HSA + NCDs mixture solution. [HSA] = 0.48×10−6 mol/L; [NCDs] = 4.56×10−6 mol/L.
Raman spectra of HSA in the absence and presence of NCDs. [HSA] = 2.41×10-6 mol/L; [NCDs] = 3.04×10-6 mol/L.
CD spectra of HSA in the absence and presence of NCDs. [HSA] =1.65×10−7 mol/L; [NCDs] (from a to c) = 0, 1.65×10−6 and 3.30×10−6 mol/L, respectively.

Synchronous fluorescence spectra of HSA with various amounts of NCDs (pH 7.40 and T = 298K). [HSA] = 2.0×10−6 mol/L; [NCDs] (from a to g) = 0, 3.0, 6.1, 9.1, 12.2, 15.2 and 18.2×10−6 mol/L, respectively.
3D fluorescence spectra of HSA (left) and the HSA-NCDs system (right) and the corresponding contour diagrams. [HSA] = 2.4×10−6 mol/L; [NCDs] = 3.0×10−6 mol/L.
Fluorescence decay fitting parameters for the HSA–NCDs system.
The binding constants K a, the number of binding sites n and the thermodynamic parameters for the HSA–NCDs system at three different temperatures.
Three-dimensional fluorescence spectral characteristics of HSA and HAS+NCDs.

head.ce:title: One-pot solid phase pyrolysis synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots and the interaction with human serum albumin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jingzhou Products Quality Supervision and Testing Institute, Jinzhou 121000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jingzhou Products Quality Supervision and Testing Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One kind of fluorescent nitrogen–doped carbon dots (denoted as NCDs) was successfully fabricated by a simple and facile one–pot solid phase pyrolysis process using citric acid and glycine as raw materials. The obtained NCDs under the optimal synthetic conditions have a fluorescence quantum yield up to 35.6% and show a spherical shape with an average diameter of about 3.0nm. Moreover, NCDs can emit strong blue fluorescence and the surfaces when excited at 345nm and their surfaces were decorated with lots of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups. In order to further elucidate the effect of NCDs to protein, steady state/time-resolved fluorescence, UV–vis absorption, circular dichroism (CD), and Raman spectroscopic techniques as well as cyclic voltammetry (CV) were employed to explore the interaction of the as-synthesized NCDs with human serum albumin (HSA) in the simulated physiological environment. The experimental data reveal that NCDs could quench the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA via the formation of a ground-state complex with the association constants of the order of 104 L/mol. The values of thermodynamic parameters obtained at three different temperatures suggest that the binding reaction of NCDs with HSA mainly driven by hydrophobic forces and hydrogen bonds was spontaneous. Furthermore, the displacement experiments confirm that the binding of NCDs primarily took place in site I of HSA. The average distance between NCDs and tryptophan (Trp) residue of HSA was estimated to be 2.3nm according to the theory of Förster non-radiation energy transfer. Finally, the analysis of synchronous fluorescence, three-dimensional fluorescence (3D), Raman, and CD spectra manifests that HSA underwent some conformational changes by unfolding the polypeptides of protein and increasing the polarity of the microenvironment surrounding both Trp and tyrosine (Tyr) residues in the presence of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:para: As the main serum protein in the circulatory system of human blood, HSA is composed of three structurally similar domains (I, II, and III), and each domain can be divided into subdomains A and B. As is known to all, the primarily regions of ligand binding to HSA are situated in hydrophobic cavities in subdomains IIA and IIIA on HSA, namely so-called site I and site II [1]. HSA molecules possess lots of important physiological functions including transportation, distribution, and delivery of endogenous and exogenous substances [2]. The ligands binding to HSA can significantly affect many properties of ligands in vivo, such as absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, stability and toxicity [3]. Moreover, the interactions of these ligands with HSA may also alter the protein structure and affect the normal physiological functions of protein [4,5].

Xu et al. firstly discovered fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) in 2004 [6]. As new quantum-sized carbon-containing fluorescent nanoparticles, CDs have exhibited promising applications in drug delivery, bio-sensing and bio-imaging because they possess many attractive properties, such as excellent optical performances, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [7,8]. Therefore, it is imperative to study the effects of CDs on proteins or cells in biological systems. To the best of our knowledge, there are many reports on the cytotoxicity of CDs to cells [9,10], but relatively few studies on the interactions of CDs with proteins [11,12]. Fluorescent CDs obtained via different synthetic methods and raw materials possess not only different optical properties, but also different particle sizes and surface chemical properties, so each kind of CDs will exhibit different effects on protein structure and function. Study on the interaction of CDs with HSA will help us to better understand the pharmacokinetic behavior of CDs and further elucidate their biological effects on protein at molecular level.

In the present paper, nitrogen–doped carbon dots were successfully synthesized by one–pot solid state pyrolysis process using citric acid and glycine as carbon and nitrogen resources. The morphology, chemical structure and optical properties of the synthesized NCDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) techniques, X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. And then the binding of NCDs to HSA was systemically investigated by spectroscopic and electrochemical methods. The fluorescence quenching mechanism and binding characteristics were analyzed. The conformational change of HSA induced by NCDs was also explored. The study is expected to provide some useful information for the safe applications of NCDs in biomedical fields.

Citric acid, glycine, Tris, hydrochloric acid (HCl), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (China). HSA (fatty acid free, purity > 98%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co., Ltd. (Milwaukee, WI, USA) and used without further purification. Phenylbutazone and ibuprofen were obtained from the National Institute for Food and Drug Control (China). All other chemicals used in this experiment are of analytical reagent grade. Double-distilled deionized water was used throughout.

In order to obtain the highly fluorescent NCDs, three main influence factors including reaction temperature, heating time, and the dosage of glycine, which will affect the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the synthesized product, have been optimized by single-factor test.

Typically, 0.50g of citric acid and 0.60g glycine were completely mixed, and then transferred into a 50mL Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave. Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave was heated at 200°C for 3h in a constant temperature drying oven. Afterwards, the vessel was allowed to cool down to room temperature. In order to remove the excess precursors and other impurities, the obtained brown solution was filtered with a 0.22µm filter membrane, and then further purified by dialysis for 2 days. Finally, the solid power of product was obtained by freeze drying and stored at 4°C before further experiments.
The molar concentration of NCDs solution was calculated in terms of mean molecular weight of 1500 [12].

The characterization of size and morphology for NCDs sample was performed on a JEM-100SX transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 100kV. The samples for TEM observation were prepared by drying a drop of NCDs solution dispersed on an amorphous carbon-coated copper grid. Powder XRD pattern was recorded via a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation, and the scanning range is from 10° to 80° with a scanning rate of 10°/min. All fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Cary Eclipse 300 FL spectrophotometer (Varian Company, USA) equipped with a thermostat bath and a 1.0cm quartz cell, and the silt widths for both excitation and emission monochromators were set at 5mm while the scanning rate was 1200nm/min. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Avatar 330 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) using the pressed KBr discs and the scanning wavelength number range was from 4000 to 400cm−1. XPS measurements were performed on an ESCALAB 250 XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) using Al Kα (hν = 1486.60eV) radiation. UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a TU-1800PC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian Company, USA) and 1.0cm quartz cell was used. Zeta potential measurements were carried out on a Zetasizer Nano ZS analyzer (Malvern, England). All the above measurements were performed at room temperature except where otherwise specified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral and electrochemical measurements for the interaction between NCDs and HSA
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solutions of 2.41×10−5 mol/L HSA and 9.12×10−4 mol/L NCDs were prepared in water. Only the working solution for the CD measurement was prepared with phosphate buffer solution (0.05M, pH 7.40), the other working solutions were prepared with Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.05M Tris, 0.15M NaCl, pH 7.40). The stock solutions of 2.41×10−6 mol/L phenylbutazone and 2.41×10−6 mol/L ibuprofen were prepared by dissolving appropriate amount of them in a small amount of methanol and then diluting with water.

Fluorescence titration spectra of HSA excited at 295nm were measured at 298, 304, and 310K, respectively, and the scanning wavelength range was from 300 to 500nm. 3mL of 2.41×10−6 mol/L HSA solution was titrated with successively adding 10μL of 9.12×10−4 mol/L NCDs solution with a micro-injector and the resulting concentration of NCDs changed from 0 to 1.82×10−5 mol/L with an interval of 3.04×10−6 mol/L. Each HSA + NCDs mixture solution was incubated at one specified temperature for 10min prior to spectral measurement. Furthermore, the synchronous fluorescence spectra of HSA after adding various amounts of NCDs were scanned from 220 to 350nm (Δλ = 15nm or Δλ = 60nm) at 298K. 3D fluorescence spectra of HSA solution in the absence and presence of NCDs were obtained at room temperature. The scanning range of the excitation wavelength is from 200 to 350nm with an increment of 5nm and the emission wavelength was from 200 to 600nm.

Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were obtained using a DeltaFlex time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) spectrometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon IBH Ltd., UK). The concentration of HSA was fixed at 2.41×10−6 mol/L and the concentrations of NCDs changed from 0 to 4.82×10−6 mol/L.
UV–vis absorption spectra of HSA, NCDs, and HSA + NCDs mixture solutions were respectively measured in the wavelength range from 200 to 500nm.

All CV measurements were carried out with conventional three-electrode electrochemical testing system. The working electrode was the NCDs modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Hg/HgCl2 electrode and platinum wire served as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The electrolyte solution was 5.0×10−3 mol/L [Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4- and 1.0×10−2 mol/L KCl. For NCDs/GCE preparation, 5μL of NCDs solution was dropped onto the surface of GCE (chitosan was added to the NCDs solution to increase the bonding force of NCDs on the electrode surface). After drying with cold air, the electrode was washed by water and then NCDs/GCE was obtained. For CV measurement, different amounts of HSA were added continuously into the electrolyte solution and then the mixture solution was stirred for 5min. The reaction system was incubated for 5min prior to each testing. CV was measured at a scan rate of 50mV/s. All CV measurements were repeated three times with different NCDs/GCE at room temperature. The concentration of HSA varied from 0 to 4.82×10−7 mol/L with an interval of 1.21×10−7 mol/L.
Raman spectra of HSA solution with and without NCDs were recorded on an InVia Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, USA) using a 633nm laser and the scanning range is from 2400 to 1000cm−1.

CD spectra of HSA solution (1.65×10−7 mol/L) after adding different concentrations of NCDs were scanned on a J-810 automatic recording spectropolarimeter (Jasco, Japan) in 1.0cm quartz cell and the scanning range is from 190 to 250nm. The scan rate was set at 200nm/min with a response time of 2s. Each CD spectrum was the average of three scans. The molar ratios of HSA to NCDs in the working solution were fixed at 1:0, 1:10 and 1:20, respectively.

The displacement experiments were carried out using phenylbutazone and ibuprofen as site probes. The molar ratio of NCDs to HSA in the HSA + NCDs mixture solution was kept at 5:1. The above mixture solution was then titrated with phenylbutazone or ibuprofen, and the corresponding fluorescence titration spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 295nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the prepared NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 is the TEM image and particle size histograms of the as-prepared NCDs sample, demonstrating that the obtained NCDs are mono-dispersed spherical particles with the average diameter of 2.96 ± 0.59nm. Fig. 2 displays the XRD pattern of NCDs sample, and only one broad and strong diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 25.6° was observed, indicating the existence of amorphous carbon [13].

FTIR spectroscopy is often used to identify the presence of surface groups on the surfaces of luminescent nanoparticles. The FTIR spectrum of NCDs was recorded as presented in Fig. 3. The broad and strong absorption band centered at 3386cm−1 should be ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H [13]. The absorption bands centered at 2929cm −1 and 1405cm−1 should originate from the stretching vibration of C–H and stretching vibration of C–N, respectively [14]. The broad and strong absorption band centered at 1660cm −1 should correspond to the stretching vibrations of C=O and N–H [14]. The small absorption peaks at 1172cm−1 and 1238cm−1 indicate the presence of C–O–C groups for typical graphitic carbon [15]. The small absorption peak at 1325cm−1 should be associated with the stretching vibration of C–C [16]. The small absorption peak at 1027cm−1 should be assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O [17]. In addition, the zeta potential of NCDs was measured to be − 13.7mV in aqueous medium of pH = 7.0. Therefore, it can be inferred from the above results that the surfaces of NCDs were decorated with lots of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine groups. The existence of hydrophilic groups on the surfaces of the as-synthesized NCDs is beneficial for improving the water solubility and stability of NCDs as well as generating more energy gaps [14].

In order to further characterize the surface components and groups of the synthesized NCDs, the XPS spectra of NCQDs were measured. As shown in Fig. 4a, there are two strong peaks and one weak peak, which indicate that the major elemental components of NCDs are C (65.02%), O (22.65%) and N (12.32%), respectively. The C1s curve (Fig. 4b) is composed of four peaks at 281.6eV, 282.3eV, 283.2eV and 285.3eV, which should be attributed to the C–H/C–O/C–N/C=O bonds, respectively. The N1s curve (Fig. 4c) consists of two peaks at 397.1eV and 397.8eV, which should be assigned to the C–N/N–H bonds, respectively. The O1s curve (Fig. 4d) can be decomposed in three peaks at 527.9eV, 528.7eV and 529.6eV, which should correspond to the C=O/C–H/C–O bonds, respectively. The results of XPS show the generation of nitrogen-doped CDs and also prove the existence of carboxyl and amine groups on their surfaces.

Fig. 5 is the absorption spectrum of the synthesized NCDs. Clearly, one characteristic absorption peak of carbon dots at 339nm was observed, which is ascribed to the n→π* electronic transition of the C=O [11]. Moreover, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NCDs were also recorded. As depicted in Fig. 5, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were found to be 345 and 420nm, respectively, which illustrates that the as-synthesized NCDs can emit a strong blue light. The Stokes shift for NCDs fluorescence is calculated to be 76nm, which is beneficial for the distinction of the target from the background signal when used in fluorescent bio-imaging. Fig. 6 shows the PL emission spectra of NCDs when excited with light of different wavelengths, and a red-shift of 45nm the maximum emission wavelength was found when the excitation wavelength changed from 290 to 390nm. The above excitation-dependent emission behavior indicates the existence of different sizes or different light-emitting sites on the surfaces of NCDs [15]. The obtained NCDs can emit multicolor fluorescence when excited with different wavelengths, this property is quite important in some potential applications. Finally, using quinine sulfate in 0.1N sulfuric acid as a standard, the quantum yield of the present NCDs was determined to be 35.6%, which demonstrates that NCDs possess excellent PL performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fluorescence quenching mechanism of HSA by NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA originates from Trp, Tyr and phenylalanine (Phe) residues in HSA [18], and the corresponding fluorescence intensity is widely used to study interactions of HSA with drugs and other substances [4,12,18].

Upon excitation at 295nm, the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA is primarily contributed to Trp214 residue alone in HSA [18], which was used to probe the binding reaction of NCDs with HSA in the present study. The fluorescence emission spectra of HSA titrated with different concentrations of NCDs were recorded and the corresponding intensity at 337nm was monitored for the HSA–NCDs system. As shown in Fig. 7, a gradual decrease in the PL intensity of HSA along with increasing NCDs concentration was observed, implying that NCDs interacted with HSA and could effectively quench the intrinsic fluorescence of Trp214 residue in protein.

Fluorescence quenching refer to any process that decreases the quantum yield of fluorescence from fluorophores. Usually fluorescence quenching includes two quenching modes: dynamic quenching results from the collisional encounters between the fluorophore and quencher, whereas static quenching is due to the formation of a fluorophore–quencher complex [11].

As seen from Fig. 5, an obvious absorption band around 340nm was observed for free NCDs solution, so the PL intensity of HSA at 337nm has been corrected for the related quantitative analysis. The fluorescence intensity can be corrected for absorption of exciting light and re-absorption of emitted light using Eq. (1) as follows [19]:
(1)
F
c o r
=
F
o b s
×
e
(
A
e x
+
A
e m
) / 2

In Eq. (1), F
cor and F obs are the corrected and observed fluorescence intensity at 337nm, respectively; A
ex and A
em are the absorbance of the HSA + NCDs mixture solution at 295nm and 337nm, respectively.

Fluorescence quenching mechanism for the HSA–NCDs system was analyzed using well known Stern–Volmer equation [19]:
(2)
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
In Eq. (2), F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of NCDs, respectively; K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of NCDs.

Fig. 8 shows the Stern–Volmer plots of K SV against [NCDs] at 298, 304 and 310K, and the corresponding values of K SV for the HSA–NCDs system were evaluated to be 4.177×104, 3.991×104 and 3.800×104 L/mol, respectively. Obviously, each plot of Stern–Volmer equation exhibits a good linear relationship in the concentration range of NCDs and the values of K SV decreased with rising temperature, suggesting that the possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of HSA by NCDs should be a static quenching process by the formation of HSA-NCDs complex.

Besides, fluorescence quenching mechanism can be further confirmed by the analysis of Stern-Volmer plots at different concentrations of HSA. The value of K SV is expected to depend on the concentration variation of HSA in a static quenching process [20]. As depicted in Fig. 9, when the concentration of HSA solution was fixed at 3.62×10−6 and 4.82×10−6 mol/L, the corresponding K SV values for the HSA–NCDs system at 298K were determined to be 6.23×104 and 5.96×104 L/mol, respectively. A discernable change in the values of K SV reconfirmed that the fluorescence quenching of HSA was initiated by a static quenching process.

UV–vis absorption measurements are often used to probe the formation of the ligand-protein complex [21]. Dynamic quenching does not cause any changes in the absorption spectrum of fluorophore, but static quenching often alter the absorption spectrum of fluorophore [12,22]. After the absorption spectra of HSA + NCDs mixture solutions and the corresponding concentration of NCDs solutions were measured, the difference absorption spectra between HSA + NCDs and NCDs solutions were obtained as depicted in Fig. 10. Clearly, there are two absorption bands: a strong one centered at 208nm and a weak one centered at 280nm, corresponding to the absorptions of the protein backbone (C=O) and the amino acid residues of HSA, respectively [23]. Obviously, the absorption spectrum of free HSA did not significantly superimpose the difference absorption spectra within the experimental errors, and the fact is that the successive addition of NCDs caused a gradual enhancement of the HSA absorption band at 208nm along with an obvious red-shift of the peak position. These results indicate that the HSA–NCDs complexes have formed, reconfirming the static quenching mechanism of HSA by NCDs.

The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of fluorophore is the most effective parameter for distinguishing the static from dynamic fluorescence quenching processes. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements for HSA in the absence and presence of NCDs were conducted and the corresponding decay curves were depicted in Fig. 11. Each curve was well-fitted to a multi-exponential function. The average lifetime (τavg) were calculated by the following equation [24]:
(3)
τ
avg
=
A
1
τ
1
+
A
2
τ
2
A
1
+
A
2
where A1 and A2, τ1 and τ2 are the relative amplitudes and the corresponding decay time constants of the individual components in biexponential decay profiles, respectively. All parameters in Eq. (3) were calculated and listed in Table 1. It was found that the average fluorescence lifetime (τavg) for HSA alone was 5.47ns, and the fluorescence lifetimes were 5.40ns and 5.33ns in the presence of 2.41×10−6 and 4.82mol/L NCDs, respectively. A slight variation in the values of τavg should be attributed to the experimental error and the expose of Trp residues to polar environment [25]. Therefore, the above results further prove the static fluorescence quenching mode of HSA-NCDs system, which is coincident with the fluorescence titration analysis.

CV is an effective and widely used method to study the binding reaction between HSA and ligands [22,26]. Fig. 12 displays the CV curves of the NCDs/GCE electrodes in varying HSA concentrations. It is apparent from this figure that a good electrochemical response of [Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4- with a pair of reversible redox peaks existed on both bare GCE and CDs/GCE. The decrease of the peak current of both redox peaks illustrates the decrease of the electron-transfer rate of [Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4- and the conductivity of NCDs/GCE. Afterwards, the NCDs/GCE was used as the working electrode to explore the interaction between NCDs and HSA. As indicated in Fig. 12, an obvious decrease in the redox peak currents and a weak shift in the redox peak potentials took place upon adding HSA into the electrolyte solution, which is due to the interaction between HSA and NCDs that decreased the diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4- subsequently [27]. The above results prove the complex formation between HSA and NCDs on the surface of GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding constants and number of binding sites for the binding of NCDs to HSA
ce:section.ce:para:
Binding constant (K a) and number of binding sites (n) of quencher molecules with HSA can be estimated by the following double-logarithmic equation [28]:
(4)
lg
F
0
− F
F
=
lg
K
a
+ n
lg
[ Q ]
where, F 0, F, and [Q] have the same meaning as in Eq. (1). As given in Fig. 13, the values of K
a and n for the HSA–NCDs system at 298, 304, and 310K were calculated from the intercept and slope of the plot of log (F
0 -F)/F versus log [NCDs] and summarized in Table 2. The values of R 2 for three regression plots are larger than 0.97, indicating that NCDs bound to HSA agrees well with the site-binding model underlined in Eq. (4). The values of n were calculated to be about 1, indicating that NCDs and HSA have formed the HSA–NCDs complex at a molar ratio of 1:1. Additionally, as listed in Table 1, the magnitudes of K
a at three different temperatures were in the range of 104, demonstrating that the strong interaction between NCDs and HSA [29], consequently they can be effectively stored and transported in the blood system through bound to HSA.

The acting forces between compounds and biomolecules may primarily include hydrophobic interaction force, hydrogen bond, van der Waals force, electrostatic force, and so on [30]. In order to further clarify the binding mode of HSA with NCDs, the thermodynamic parameters for the HSA–NCDs system at 298, 304 and 310K, including enthalpy change (ΔH), entropy changes (ΔS), and free energy change (ΔG) were estimated using following Eqs. (5) and (6). If the value of ΔH does not vary too much over the temperature range investigated, the values of ΔH and ΔS can be evaluated using the van’t Hoff equation [31]:
(5)
ln
K
a
= −
Δ
H
R T
+
Δ
S
R

In Eq. (5), R is the idea gas constant, K
a is the binding constant at the corresponding temperature T.

Firstly, the values of ΔH, ΔS and ΔG at each temperature were evaluated from the slope and intercept of the curve of ln K a versus 1/T as presented in Fig. 14. Afterwards, the values of ΔG were obtained from the following expression [31]:
(6)
Δ
G =
Δ
H − T
Δ
S

As summarized in Table 2, the values of ΔH and ΔS for the HSA–NCDs system at 298, 304 and 310K were calculated. ΔG < 0 indicate that the binding reaction between NCDs and HSA spontaneously occurred. Moreover, according to the rules of binding mode [30], ΔS > 0 is regularly regarded as an evidence of hydrophobic interaction and ΔH < 0 can be primarily attributed to hydrogen bonds. Therefore, hydrophobic interactions and hydrogen bonds in stabilizing the HSA–NCDs complex played a predominate role. The FTIR analysis in Section 3.1 have proved that there exist lots of carboxyl, hydroxy and amine groups on the surfaces of the present NCDs. These oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups can form hydrogen bonds with O or N in the amino acid residues of HSA, so these groups play important role in the binding reaction.

Besides, the zeta potentials of HSA and NCDs were measured to be − 3.8 and − 5.6mV in aqueous solution, respectively, indicating that both of them carry negatively charged at pH = 7.40. Thus, the electrostatic repulsion between HSA and NCDs cannot be completely excluded, resulting in weakening the binding affinity between NCDs and HSA.

The binding distance (r) between protein residue (donor) and ligand (acceptor) can be estimated according to the theory of Förster's non-radiative energy transfer [32]. The efficiency of energy transfer (E), the binding distance (r) and the critical energy transfer distance (R 0) between donor and acceptor can be expressed by:
(7)
E = 1 −
F
F
0
=
R
0
6
(
R
0
6
+
r
6
)
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of donor before and after the addition of acceptor, respectively. The value of R 0 can be calculated using the following equation [33]:
(8)
R
0
6
= 8.8 ×
10
− 25
( J
k
2
φ
n
− 4
)
where k 2 is the orientation factor related to the geometry of the donor and acceptor of dipoles; n is the averaged refracted index of the medium in the wavelength range where spectral overlap is significant; φ is the fluorescence quantum yield of donor; J is the spectral overlap integral between the fluorescence emission spectrum of donor and the absorption spectrum of acceptor. The value of J can be evaluated using the following expression [34]:
(9)
J =
∫ 0 ∞
F ( λ ) ε ( λ )
λ
4
d λ
∫ 0 ∞
F ( λ ) d λ
In Eq. (9), F (λ) and ε(λ) are the fluorescence intensity of donor and the molar absorption coefficient of acceptor, respectively.

The spectral overlap of the absorption spectrum of NCDs and the PL spectrum of HSA was shown in Fig. 15. Firstly, the value of J was obtained by integrating the overlap (yellow shade); and then the value of R 0 was calculated by Eq. (8) using k 2 = 2/3, n = 1.336, and φ = 0.118 for HSA [35]; finally, the value of r was estimated to be 2.3nm using Eq. (7) according to J = 7.363×10−16 cm3 L/mol, E = 9.0%, R
0 = 1.6nm. The average binding distance r between Trp214 and NCDs is shorter than 7nm, illustrating the occurrence of the non-radiative energy transfer from HSA to NCDs with a high possibility [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Localization of NCDs binding site on HSA
ce:section.ce:para:
When ligand L 1 and ligand L 2 bind to one HSA molecule simultaneously, following two types of interaction may take place [37]:
(i)
competitive binding
L
1
⇒
+
HSA
L
1
−
HSA
⇒
+
L
2
L
2
−
HSA
+
L
1
(ii)
non-competitive binding
L
1
⇒
+
HSA
L
1
−
HSA
⇒
+
L
2
L
2
−
HSA
−
L
1

In order to identify the location of NCDs binding site on HSA, the displacement experiments were conducted with two site probes, phenylbutazone (site I) and ibuprofen (site II) [38]. The displacement of NCDs by probe can be quantified via the percentage of probe displacement according to the method proposed by Sudlow et al. [1].
(10)
P r o b e
d i s p l a c e m e n t
( % ) = F /
F
0
× 100 %
In Eq. (10), F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of HSA + NCDs mixture solution before and after the addition of probe.

Based on the spectral data obtained from the displacement experiments, the plots of F/F 0 vs. site probe concentration were drawn as displayed in Fig. 16. Obviously, the values of F/F 0 significantly decrease by successively adding phenylbutazone, while the addition of ibuprofen show a negligible influence on the PL intensity of the same HSA + NCDs mixture solution. It can be inferred from the above results that phenylbutazone completed with NCDs for the same binding site, while ibuprofen did not displace NCDs from HSA. Hence, we can draw that NCDs is likely to be bound to site I (sub-domain II A) on HSA.

Raman spectroscopy is often used to probe the protein structural changes upon binding with ligands [39,40]. The Raman spectra of HSA with and without NCDs were recorded and depicted in Fig. 17. The peak at 1644cm−1 should belong primarily to the C=O stretching vibration of amide I, which is sanative to the secondary structure of the protein [38]; the peak at 1538cm−1 should be attributed to the C–H stretching vibration and N–H bending vibration of amide II; the peak at 1382cm−1 should be related to the C–H bending vibration [41]. As seen from this figure, obviously the intensities of these three peaks were enhanced in the presence of NCDs, implying that the addition of NCDs altered the secondary structure of the protein.

CD spectroscopy as a valuable method is often used to examine the secondary structure of proteins in solution [40]. In the present study, the CD spectra of HSA in the presence of 0, 1.65×10−6, and 3.3×10−6 mol/L NCDs were recorded, respectively. As presented in Fig. 18, there are two obvious characteristic peaks of α-helical structure at 208nm and 220nm for the π→π* and n→π* transition, respectively, which reflect the characteristic of the α-helical structure in proteins [38,42].

The mean residue ellipticity (MRE) in degcm2 dmol−1 and the α-helix contents of free and bound HSA were calculated using the following equations [35]:
(11)
MRE =
Observed
CD
( mdeg )
C
P
n l × 10
(12)
α - H e l i x
( % ) =
−
MRE
208
− 4000
33 , 000 − 4000
× 100
where C
p is the molar concentration of HSA, n is the number of amino acid residues, l is the path length and MRE208 is the MRE value at 208nm. It was found that the α-helical content decreased from 56.6% in free HSA to 53.6% and 49.5% at the molar ratio of HSA to NCDs ranging from 1:10 to 1:20. The results of the above CD spectra illustrate that NCDs bound with the amino acid residues on the main polypeptide chain of HSA and destroyed the hydrogen bonding networks, resulting in changing the secondary structure of protein and disrupting its native conformation [1,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The polarity of the region around Trp and Tyr in HSA
ce:section.ce:para: Synchronous fluorescence is simple and sensitive approach for probing the microenvironment of amino acid residues. The red-shift or blue-shift in the maximum emission wavelength of fluorescence is closely related to the changes of the polarity around the chromophores [11]. When the difference between excitation wavelength and emission wavelength is kept at 15 or 60nm, the synchronous fluorescence of HSA will provide the characteristic information of Tyr or Trp residues in HSA [44].

The synchronous fluorescence titration spectra of HSA after adding different concentrations of NCDs were performed. It can be seen from Fig. 19 that the fluorescence intensity gradually declined along with the addition of NCDs, and the corresponding maximum emission wavelength underwent a slight red-shift of 1.3nm (from 286nm to 287.3nm) when Δλ = 15nm and a slight red-shift of 2nm (from 274nm to 276nm) when Δλ = 60nm. These results indicate that the interaction of NCDs with HSA quenched the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA, the polarity surrounding both Trp and Tyr residues was increased, and namely the hydrophobicity was decreased. The major reason should be that the binding of NCDs caused the slight collapse of the hydrophobic structure surrounding the Trp and Tyr residues, consequently these residues buried in the hydrophobic cavity in HSA were exposed more to the aqueous phase.

3D fluorescence is a rapid and powerful approach to study the conformational change of protein because many fluorescence characteristics can be acquired by changing excitation and emission wavelength simultaneously [38,45].

The 3D fluorescence spectra of HSA in the presence of 0 and 3.0×10−6 mol/L NCDs were measured as presented in Fig. 18, and the related characteristic parameters were summarized in Table 3. As can be seen from this figure, two strong fluorescence peaks were observed: peak 1 at 280nm primarily reflects the spectral characteristics of both Trp and Tyr residues; peak 2 at 230nm primarily displays the fluorescence spectral behavior of the polypeptide backbone structure of HSA, originating from the transition of π–π* of HSA's characteristic polypeptide backbone structure C=O [11]; peaks 3 and 4 belong to the Rayleigh scattering peak (λex = λem) and the second-ordered scattering peak (λem = 2λex), respectively. As given in Table 3, the fluorescence intensities of both peak 1 and peak 2 were declined by 20.8% and 17.5% in the of NCDs, respectively. In addition, the intensity at both peaks 3 and 4 in Fig. 20 obviously enhanced after adding NCDs, manifesting that there existed some large-sized particles in the HSA + NCDs mixture solution. The enhancement of the Rayleigh scattering peaks should be ascribed to the formation of large-sized HSA-NCDs complexes, which also provides another evidence for the static quenching of HSA by NCDs. The above results indicate that the binding interaction of NCDs with HSA caused some conformational alternations in HSA structure, which is in accordance with synchronous fluorescence, CD, Raman, and UV–vis absorption spectral analysis.

Water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots with a quantum yield of 35.6% have been synthesized by one–step solid state approach using citric acid and glycine raw materials. The obtained NCDs have an average diameter of approximately 3.0nm and the surfaces are decorated with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups. Several spectroscopic techniques in combination with CV were used to explore the interaction of NCDs with HSA. It was found that the probable quenching mechanism of HSA by NCDs is a static quenching process through a complex formation at 1:1M ratio, which was verified by the CV curves and lifetime measurements. The values of thermodynamic parameters suggest that hydrophobic forces and hydrogen bond splayed major roles in stabilizing the HSA–NCDs complex. The value of the binding distance r reveals that the energy transfer from HSA to NCDs occurred with a high possibility. The displacement experiments indicate that sub-domain IIA (site I) of HSA is the main binding site for NCDs. Furthermore, the results of synchronous fluorescence, Raman, CD and 3D fluorescence spectra demonstrate that NCDs bound to HSA changed the secondary structure of HSA as well the hydrophobicity around Trp and Tyr residues, suggesting that the as-synthesized NCDs may disturb the normal functions of protein to some extent when used in biomedical field. The study may provide some valuable information for evaluating the effects of NCDs to organisms.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support of Liaoning Provincial Department of Education Innovation Team Projects (Grant no. LT2012001), Shenyang Science and Technology Plan Project (Grant no. F13289100) and China Association for Medical Education (2016SKT-M019). The authors also thank their colleagues and other students who participated in this work.

ce:section.ce:para: There are no conflict of interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The characterization of two specific drug binding sites on human serum albumin

Facile and green approach to prepare fluorescent carbon dots: emergent nanomaterial for cell imaging and detection of vitamin B2
